Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Thomas Brown

1778 – 1820

A Scottish philosopher-physician on perception, association, and the reform of common sense.

I take “Thomas Brown” here to mean Dr Thomas Brown (1778–1820), the Scottish physician, poet, and philosopher who held the Chair of Moral Philosophy at Edinburgh and whose posthumous Lectures on the Philosophy of the Human Mind became a major early nineteenth-century text in British and American philosophy of mind.

1. Central thesis

Brown’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a law-governed, introspective science of mental states, joined to a minimal but real spiritual-substance dualism. He is neither a crude associationist who dissolves the mind into a mere bundle of ideas, nor a traditional faculty psychologist who treats “memory,” “imagination,” “will,” “judgment,” and “consciousness” as quasi-independent inner agencies. His mature view is this: the only proper scientific data of mental philosophy are the mind’s successive “states,” “affections,” or “feelings”; the business of psychology is to classify these states, analyze complex states into simpler elements, and discover the laws by which one state succeeds another. Yet these states are not free-floating events. They are states of a continuing subject, a mind or thinking principle, whose essence is unknown but whose identity is immediately and irresistibly believed. Brown therefore combines a Humean, empiricist psychology of succession with a Reid-like appeal to intuitive belief in personal or mental identity, while sharply reducing Reid’s multiplication of mental faculties.

This makes Brown historically important as a transitional figure. He stands after Hume, Reid, and Dugald Stewart, but before Alexander Bain, John Stuart Mill’s mature logic of causation, nineteenth-century associationist psychology, and later empirical psychology. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes Brown as the figure who moved Scottish philosophy “into a new phase”: he was Stewart’s student, but more independent, more sympathetic to Humean empiricism, critical of Reid, and influential through lectures that were widely read in Britain and America before falling into neglect after mid-century.

2. Brown’s project: the “physiology of mind”

Brown calls the philosophy of mind not merely a speculative inquiry into the intellectual part of human nature, but also a practical science tied to duty, hope, destiny, and moral cultivation. Yet, methodologically, he wants to treat the mind as an object of “physical” inquiry in the older sense: not materialist physics, but an empirical, law-seeking investigation of phenomena. The mind is to be studied by observing its phenomena, analyzing complex states, and tracing the order of their sequences. He explicitly compares this to the inquiry into material phenomena, not because he thinks mind is matter, but because both inquiries require disciplined observation and the rejection of empty scholastic abstractions.

This methodological commitment is central. Brown thinks earlier metaphysics went wrong when it treated philosophy of mind as an inquiry into hidden essences, entities, quiddities, and verbal abstractions. His criticism of scholastic debates is severe: he mocks questions about whether the mind’s essence is distinct from its existence, whether will is an entity or quiddity, and whether angels can occupy a vacuum or pass through space without traversing intermediate points. Against this, he insists that the mind is best known through the “series of changes” it presents and through the circumstances that precede and follow those changes.

Brown’s “physiology of mind” is thus not physiology in the modern neuroscientific sense. It is a descriptive, analytic, and causal science of mental phenomena. Its object is not the brain, and not the metaphysical essence of the soul, but the regularities of thought, perception, memory, imagination, emotion, desire, and belief as they appear in consciousness. In modern terms, Brown is closer to a phenomenological psychologist of mental succession than to either a neurophysiologist or a speculative metaphysician.

3. Ontology: mind as unknown substance, known through states

Brown’s ontology begins from a disciplined distinction: we do not know the essence of mind, just as we do not know the essence of matter. We know mind “relatively,” through the succession of feelings of which we are conscious; we know matter relatively, through the phenomena it exhibits. What is known in both cases is not bare substance but the ordered presentation of states, qualities, or phenomena. Still, Brown does not infer that mind is a fiction. Rather, he holds that by our constitution we cannot help ascribing these phenomena to some permanent subject.

This is the core of Brown’s anti-bundle position. A Humean bundle theorist may treat the mind as only a succession of perceptions. Brown accepts the succession as the only object of scientific inspection, but he refuses to identify the mind with the succession alone. “Mind” is the permanent subject of perception, memory, reason, joy, grief, love, hate, and the rest. Its essence remains inaccessible; every new act of knowing the mind would itself be only another mental phenomenon. But the existence of a subject of the phenomena is, for Brown, unavoidable.

This position is a form of minimal substance dualism plus epistemic humility. Brown does not claim to describe what the spiritual substance is in itself. He rejects the idea that we can penetrate the “essence” of mind by dialectic. But he also rejects the reduction of mental phenomena to matter. Mind is a permanent subject of states; matter is a permanent subject of a different class of phenomena. The two are known by different presentations, and the identity of the thinking principle is not, for Brown, explicable by the flux of bodily particles.

A crucial consequence follows: Brown’s basic mental ontology is not a faculty ontology. The mind does not contain many little agencies called memory, imagination, will, understanding, consciousness, and so forth. It is one subject capable of many states. To speak of “faculties” can be convenient, but it becomes philosophically dangerous when generic names are reified. Brown explicitly warns that when philosophers divide states into “will” and “understanding,” they often convert these names into competing powers, almost as if each faculty were a smaller mind inside the mind.

4. “Feelings,” “states,” and “affections”: Brown’s mental vocabulary

Brown uses “feeling” in an unusually broad sense. It does not mean merely emotion, pleasure, pain, or bodily sensation. It means any conscious mental occurrence: sensations, perceptions, thoughts, desires, memories, emotions, judgments, and conceptions. This broad use is not incidental; it is his way of avoiding the traditional separation between “cognitive” and “affective” mental life. All are modes in which the mind exists.

His preferred technical language is “state” or “affection” of mind. A state is the mind’s momentary mode of being; an affection is the same state considered as arising after certain antecedent circumstances. This gives Brown a unified event-like ontology of mental life: sensations, thoughts, emotions, memories, desires, judgments, and acts of will are all successive states of a single subject. This is one of the most modern features of his thought. Instead of asking which faculty produced an inner act, he asks what kind of state it is, what simpler states it contains, and by what laws it follows from prior states.

This state-ontology also explains Brown’s resistance to “ideas” as inner objects. Later interpreters correctly stress that Brown treats ideas as states of the mind itself, not as objects in an inner theater. Because the mind can occupy only one state at a time, complex ideas cannot literally be heaps of simple ideas sitting together inside consciousness. Their complexity must be explained by succession, condensation, virtual coexistence, relation, and the mind’s power of being modified in determinate ways.

5. Consciousness: no separate inner faculty

Brown’s account of consciousness is one of his most important anti-faculty doctrines. Consciousness, in its simplest sense, is not a separate power that observes mental states. It is not an inner eye directed upon thoughts, pleasures, pains, or desires as objects. Rather, “consciousness” is a general term for the present existence of any mental state whatever. To be conscious of a sensation is not to have a sensation plus a second act of consciousness; it is simply to have the sensation.

This is a powerful anti-homuncular move. Brown denies that every mental event must be doubled by a second mental act that takes it as object. If such doubling were required, we would either face an infinite regress or turn consciousness into a mysterious faculty. Brown’s position is more economical: the presence of the mental state is its being conscious. When we speak of “the evidence of consciousness,” we mean only the immediate impossibility of both having and not having the present sensation, thought, or desire.

However, Brown distinguishes present consciousness from retrospective self-reference. When consciousness is taken to mean more than the present feeling—when it involves the thought that this feeling belongs to me, the same subject who had former feelings—it already includes memory and the intuitive belief of identity. Thus consciousness in the strict sense is momentary; consciousness in the richer personal sense involves a present state, remembrance of a past state, and the belief that both are states of one subject.

This is a subtle distinction. Brown avoids both an inflated “consciousness faculty” and a deflationary denial of selfhood. The self is not an object separately inspected by consciousness; it is the permanent subject to which remembered and present states are irresistibly referred. This gives Brown a theory of self-consciousness without a Cartesian inner theater and without a Humean reduction of self to a bundle.

6. Mental identity and anti-materialism

Brown’s doctrine of mental identity is one of the most explicit places where his philosophy of mind becomes metaphysical. He distinguishes strict mental identity from ordinary personal identity. “Person,” in ordinary usage, includes bodily continuity and social identity as well as mind. But the strict philosophical issue is the identity of the thinking principle itself. This identity, Brown argues, must be mental if it is absolute, because the body is in continual material flux.

The evidence for mental identity is not inferential. Brown thinks we believe ourselves to be the same mind today as yesterday by an immediate, irresistible principle of our nature. This belief is not produced by a chain of reasoning; it is one of the intuitive principles on which reasoning itself depends. A skeptical argument against such first principles would have to use reasoning, thereby presupposing some principle of validity it cannot itself establish by proof.

This is where Brown sides with Reid against Humean skepticism, even while remaining more Humean than Reid in his psychology. He accepts “intuitive beliefs,” but he tries to limit them. He does not want a lavish appeal to common sense whenever an explanation is difficult. But he does hold that some beliefs—mental identity, expectation of similar sequences, the practical belief in an external world—are primitive features of our constitution.

Brown’s anti-materialism depends on this identity doctrine. If the thinking principle is absolutely one and the same through bodily change, then thought and feeling cannot be merely the result of material organization, at least not if matter is understood as divisible, changing, and lacking strict identity through flux. He treats the unity and permanence of the mind as a strong argument against materialist systems. This is not a fully developed modern argument against physicalism, but it is a clear nineteenth-century argument from indivisible mental identity to the inadequacy of material composition.

The tension is obvious but important. Brown’s official method says: know only the phenomena and their laws. His metaphysics says: the phenomena belong to an identical spiritual subject. He reconciles these by appeal to natural belief: we do not know the essence of the subject, but we cannot help believing in it. Thus his dualism is not a speculative map of the soul’s inner constitution. It is a restrained, common-sense, identity-based dualism attached to an empiricist science of mental states.

7. Soul, immortality, and incorporeal minds

Brown freely uses the language of “soul,” “spiritual substance,” “immortal soul,” “thinking principle,” and “beneficent Creator.” The human mind is not merely an organism’s functional behavior; it is a spiritual subject whose moral and intellectual development is tied to human destiny. In the opening lecture, he frames mental philosophy as concerned with duties, hopes, and the “great destiny” of human beings, and with the mind as an instrument of good under a Creator.

His references to immortality are not incidental. In his discussion of the condensation and virtual coexistence of feelings, he presents intellectual and moral progress in this life as a pledge of a nobler progression in ages to come; he also describes generous affections as preparing us for purer communion and as elements of happiness rewarded through immortality. This confirms that Brown’s psychology is embedded in a theistic moral anthropology, even though his technical method is empirical and analytic.

At the same time, Brown is hostile to speculative angelology and to unsupported claims about incorporeal beings beyond the human mind. He remarks that nature gives no evidence for the existence of “spiritual ministers” such as angels, and that revelation teaches little beyond their existence. He ridicules scholastic debates about angels’ motion, vision in darkness, coexistence in a point, and existence in a vacuum. This is philosophically significant: Brown’s acceptance of the human soul and divine order does not license a general speculative metaphysics of incorporeal beings.

The best reconstruction is therefore: Brown is a Christian or theistic dualist about the human mind, but an empiricist and anti-scholastic about what can be known. He affirms an immortal spiritual subject, but he refuses to treat the philosophy of mind as a science of hidden immaterial essences. The soul is real, but its scientific study proceeds through the succession of its states.

8. Causation: Brown’s regularity theory and its importance for psychology

Brown’s general theory of causation is essential to his philosophy of mind. In An Inquiry into the Relation of Cause and Effect, he argues that causation contains no discoverable mysterious tie beyond invariable antecedence and consequence. A cause is that which immediately precedes a change and which, in similar circumstances, has always been and will always be followed by a similar change. “Power” is not an occult inner force; it is a name for the antecedent considered in relation to the invariability of its consequent.

This theory matters for the mind because Brown applies the same anti-occult analysis to mental causation. If one feeling is followed by another, or if volition is followed by bodily movement, we do not perceive a special metaphysical power. We perceive, or believe in, a sequence of feeling after feeling, or feeling after bodily event, under regular conditions. If causation means uniform antecedence and consequence, then it applies to mental phenomena; if it means something more, Brown says we do not discover that “something more” in the mind any more than in matter.

This is one reason Brown is sometimes treated as a more consistent Humean than Hume on causation. Hume famously connected causation with constant conjunction and custom, but Brown rejects the idea that custom is required to generate the expectation of uniformity. For Brown, the belief that similar antecedents will have similar consequents is an intuitive principle of anticipation. We may observe a sequence and immediately expect its recurrence in similar circumstances; this expectation is not derived from a separate inference but belongs to our constitution.

In psychology, this gives Brown a law-based program. Mental science should not look for hidden bonds among ideas or faculties. It should identify the regular circumstances under which certain states arise after others. Thus his theory of causation supports his theory of suggestion, his critique of faculties, his account of perception, and his analysis of volition.

9. Perception and the external world

Brown’s account of perception is one of his most complex negotiations between Reid and Hume. He rejects Berkeleyan idealism as psychologically unlivable, but he also criticizes Reid’s claim to have given a genuine argumentative refutation of skepticism about the external world. Brown’s famous attitude, reported by the Stanford Encyclopedia, is that Reid loudly insists we must believe in an outward world while quietly admitting we can give no reason for the belief, whereas Hume loudly insists we can give no reason while quietly admitting we cannot get rid of the belief. Brown thinks the lived force of external-world belief is not created by philosophical proof.

Brown distinguishes sensation from perception. Sensation is the mental affection itself; perception adds reference of the sensation to an external cause. But that reference does not require a special faculty of perception. It can be explained through more general principles: the belief that a new consequent requires some new antecedent, and the associative fusion of feelings that have frequently coexisted.

Touch and muscular resistance are central. Brown argues that smell, taste, hearing, and even vision by themselves would not originally give us the notion of external resisting bodies. The sense-complex associated with touch, movement, impeded muscular contraction, extension, and resistance provides the primitive basis for our conception of body. Other sensations become referred to bodies by association with these tactual-muscular experiences.

His treatment of vision is empiricist. Mere light falling on the retina does not, by itself, disclose real distance, magnitude, or three-dimensional figure. In human beings, Brown believes visual perception of distance and magnitude is largely learned through experience, though he allows that other animals may possess more instinctive visual adjustments. This places him in the tradition of learned perception and anticipates later developmental and associationist accounts of spatial perception.

The result is not skepticism. Brown says that, whatever the original genesis of the belief in an external world, it is now impossible for us to disbelieve that sensations have some external cause. The belief is part of our mental constitution as far back as philosophical inquiry can reach. Thus Brown’s view is neither naïve direct realism nor idealism. It is a psychologically grounded realism: external-world belief arises from sensation, muscular resistance, association, and intuitive causal expectation, and becomes practically irresistible.

10. Classification of mental phenomena

Brown’s classification is designed to replace the traditional division between understanding and will. He argues that the understanding/will division is incomplete and misleading because it leaves out many affective phenomena and encourages reification. His alternative begins from the causes or immediate antecedents of mental states: some arise from objects or bodily-organ changes external to the mind; others arise from prior states of the mind itself.

The first class is external affections, also called sensitive states: sensations and perceptions occasioned by external objects or by bodily organs. These are not bodily states themselves but mental states arising in connection with bodily stimulation.

The second and third classes are internal affections. Brown divides these into intellectual states and emotions. Intellectual states include remembering, imagining, comparing, and considering relations. Emotions are vivid feelings that arise when we perceive, remember, imagine, or compare: beauty, sublimity, astonishment, love, hate, hope, fear, and analogous affective states. For Brown, the whole field of consciousness can be covered by sensitive states, intellectual states, and emotions.

This classification is historically important because Brown gives “emotion” a systematic theoretical role. Thomas Dixon has argued that Brown was a key figure in the emergence of “the emotions” as the modern psychological category that gradually displaced older vocabularies of passions, affections, and sentiments. Brown did not invent the word, but he helped make “emotions” a central class in the science of mind.

11. Suggestion: Brown’s replacement for association

Brown’s best-known psychological principle is suggestion. He prefers this term to “association” because “association of ideas” suggests a mysterious link or union among ideas prior to their succession. Brown thinks this is an explanatory mistake. The fact to be explained is that, when the mind is in one state, another state often spontaneously follows. To posit an additional hidden association that produces the succession adds a mystery and may become circular.

There are two major forms: simple suggestion and relative suggestion. Simple suggestion occurs when one perception or conception is followed by another without any felt relation being part of the new state—for example, hearing a friend’s name and thinking of the friend. Relative suggestion occurs when the mind feels a relation among two or more objects or states: resemblance, difference, proportion, agreement, disagreement, before/after, cause/effect, fitness/unfitness. Brown treats judgment, in the strict sense, as a form of relative suggestion: the felt relation of objects considered together.

This is a radical simplification of the intellect. Conception after conception gives the train of thought; felt relations among conceptions give judgment and reasoning. Brown therefore reduces many supposed faculties to two generic susceptibilities: the susceptibility to have one state suggest another, and the susceptibility to feel relations among states or objects.

Brown’s theory is not merely “associationism” in the crude sense. He rejects the idea that mental states are literally tied together by associative links. He also rejects the idea that all mental life can be reduced to contiguity without remainder. He speaks of relations such as resemblance, contrast, and contiguity, and he introduces secondary laws to explain why one suggestion occurs rather than another. Later interpreters rightly note that Brown occupies a distinctive position: an associationist in explanatory ambition, but critical of traditional associationist metaphors.

12. Secondary laws of suggestion

Brown’s “secondary laws” are among his most important contributions to psychology. The primary laws explain the general relations by which states suggest one another. But a given object can suggest many possible thoughts. A portrait may suggest the sitter, the artist, the friend who gave it, the room where it hung, or events connected with it. The question is why one suggested state rather than another arises at a particular moment. Brown answers by identifying modifying circumstances: duration, liveliness, frequency, recency, emotional interest, constitutional temperament, and related factors.

This anticipates later psychological attention to attention, salience, affective intensity, habit strength, recency effects, and individual differences. Brown’s laws are not quantitative experimental laws, but they are recognizably empirical generalizations about cognitive dynamics. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Brown’s introduction of secondary laws may have been his most durable associationist legacy.

The secondary laws also show why Brown is not offering a purely mechanical sequence theory. The same antecedent state can be followed by different consequents depending on attention, affect, repetition, constitution, and context. His psychology is law-governed but probabilistic in spirit: it seeks conditions that make certain suggestions more likely, not a rigid deterministic table of thought.

13. Virtual coexistence and complex mental states

Brown faces an obvious problem: if the mind can be in only one state at a time, how can there be complex thoughts, complex emotions, or rich imaginative wholes? His answer is the doctrine of virtual coexistence. A suggesting feeling need not vanish instantly when it gives rise to another. It may continue, in a virtual sense, alongside the feelings it suggests, allowing condensation into complex wholes.

This doctrine is crucial for his account of memory, imagination, emotion, and moral development. A word such as “home” does not merely call up a single image and then disappear. It may become the center of a condensed cluster of feelings, memories, places, persons, and affections. Brown uses this to explain the force of complex emotions, especially aesthetic and moral emotions.

The theory is also a way of avoiding literal compositionality. Brown does not need to say that many ideas exist as separate parts simultaneously inside the mind. Instead, a complex state is a rapid, condensed, relationally structured modification of the mind, sustained by the lingering influence of prior states and the succession of suggested states. This is one of his most sophisticated solutions to the problem of mental complexity.

14. Memory, recollection, and imagination

Brown’s anti-faculty analysis is especially visible in his treatment of memory. Memory is not a simple power that stores and retrieves inner objects. It is a complex state: a conception plus a felt relation of priority to the present. To remember something is to have a conception as past. Time itself, in Brown’s analysis, is not a thing existing independently in consciousness but a felt relation of before and after among conceived events.

Recollection is likewise not a special faculty that voluntarily summons ideas. Brown argues that to will an idea directly would already require knowing what the idea is; and to will indirectly the associated circumstances faces the same problem if those circumstances are themselves supposedly willed. The better account is that a continuing desire to remember coexists with spontaneous trains of suggestion until the desired conception arises. Voluntary recollection is thus desire plus suggestion, not an autonomous power of retrieval.

Imagination receives a similar treatment. In its ordinary form, imagination is a mode of simple suggestion: images arise spontaneously in sequence. In artistic or deliberate composition, desire or intention keeps a subject before the mind, suggestion supplies images and combinations, and relative suggestion or judgment approves or rejects them as fit or unfit. “Imagination” is therefore a convenient name for a complex process, not an indivisible faculty.

This reductionist strategy does not eliminate the phenomena; it re-describes them. Brown is not denying memory, recollection, or imagination. He is denying that they are primitive powers. His philosophical psychology tries to show how they arise from more elementary susceptibilities: suggestion, relation, desire, and judgment.

15. Judgment, reasoning, generality, and language

Brown’s relative suggestion theory gives him a distinctive account of judgment and reasoning. Judgment is not an act of a separate faculty called “the understanding.” It is the feeling of a relation among objects of thought. Reasoning is an ordered development of such relational feelings, especially where one admitted relation leads to another.

Generality depends on the mind’s susceptibility to feel resemblance. Classification is possible because the mind feels relations of similarity among objects; the relation is not a sensory feature existing in the object in the same way as color or shape. General terms are founded on these felt resemblances and give language its power by allowing a single sign to stand for indefinitely many similar objects.

Brown does not think reasoning is impossible without language. Children, non-speaking persons, and even animals can exhibit forms of practical reasoning. But without general terms, reasoning remains limited. Language stabilizes classifications, definitions, and communicable knowledge. The whole conduct of life depends on rude classification and on the intuitive expectation that similar antecedents will yield similar consequents.

This places Brown between nominalism and psychological conceptualism. He does not treat universals as independently existing entities, nor does he reduce general thought to words alone. Generality arises from the mind’s felt relations of resemblance, and language then fixes and extends those relations for reasoning and communication.

16. Emotion and moral psychology

Brown’s classification makes emotion one of the three great divisions of mental life. Emotions are internal affections distinct from mere intellectual conceptions, though they arise upon perceptions, memories, imaginations, and comparisons. They may coexist with reasoning and desire, but they are not reducible to either. This gives affect a central place in the architecture of mind.

Historically, this is a major contribution. Dixon’s work on the history of emotion identifies Brown as a key figure in the shift from older categories such as passions, affections, and sentiments to the modern category “emotions.” Brown’s Lectures helped systematize emotion as a psychological class, and his influence extended well beyond technical metaphysics into the later vocabulary of psychology.

Brown’s emotions are also tied to moral life. The doctrine of virtual coexistence allows complex emotions to form through condensation of many prior thoughts and feelings. Moral affections, sympathy, social attachment, aesthetic responses, and religious hopes can therefore be treated as complex mental formations rather than as unanalyzable faculties. Brown’s moral psychology is affective without being anti-intellectual: emotions depend on perceptions and conceptions, but they shape conduct, virtue, happiness, and character.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Brown resists the later temptation to treat cognition as the core of mind and affect as an appendage. In his taxonomy, emotion is not secondary in importance, even when it is often secondary in origin to perception or thought. A comprehensive Brownian mind is sensitive, intellectual, and emotional at once.

17. Will, desire, and action

Brown’s theory of will follows the same analytic pattern. He is suspicious of treating “will” as a primitive faculty with a mysterious causal power. In his causation writings, he treats volition as a complex feeling or state that stands in sequence with other feelings and bodily movements. We do not discover in volition a special power beyond antecedence and consequence.

This does not mean Brown denies agency. Rather, he redescribes agency in psychological terms: desires persist, conceptions arise, relations of fitness are felt, expectations form, and bodily motions follow under the established laws of our constitution. What ordinary language calls willing is a complex of desire, belief, expectation, and subsequent action, not a metaphysical eruption of unobservable power.

This is consistent with his broader causation theory. Mental causation is not special because it reveals a hidden necessary connection. It is special only in the kind of phenomena involved: feelings, desires, thoughts, and volitions rather than motions, pressures, and material changes. The form of causal intelligibility is the same: regular sequence under similar circumstances.

18. Brown’s place in the history of philosophy of mind

Brown’s historical position is best understood through four contrasts.

First, against scholastic and rationalist essence-metaphysics, he insists that the mind must be studied through observable mental phenomena and their sequences, not through dialectical claims about hidden essences. His attack on angelography and scholastic quiddities is part of a broader move toward empirical mental science.

Second, against Reidian faculty psychology, Brown reduces the number of primitive powers. Reid and Stewart treat memory, imagination, judgment, habit, and other powers as distinct faculties. Brown thinks this multiplies explanatory entities and risks turning names into inner agents. He keeps some intuitive principles, but he rejects the architecture of many independent faculties.

Third, against Humean skeptical dissolution, Brown retains mind, self, external-world belief, and intuitive anticipation. He accepts a Humean suspicion of necessary connections and hidden powers, but not Hume’s more destabilizing consequences for personal identity and belief. In this sense, he is a mediator: Humean in method and causation, Scottish in his appeal to natural belief and the science of mind.

Fourth, before Bain and later empirical psychology, Brown gives an introspective law-psychology that helps prepare the way for psychological explanation by regularities. Bain later sharpens the distinction between psychology and metaphysics and gives priority to empirical psychology; Brown still embeds psychology in moral philosophy, theology, and metaphysical commitments about the soul. This is why Brown looks both modern and pre-modern: he is a decisive step toward psychology, but not yet a secular experimental psychologist.

19. The extracted Brownian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Brownian philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

The mind is a single, permanent, spiritual subject whose essence is unknown. It is not known by intellectual inspection of its substance, but by the successive states in which it exists. These states include sensations, perceptions, conceptions, memories, judgments, desires, volitions, and emotions. The science of mind is the analysis and classification of these states and the discovery of the laws by which they succeed one another.

Consciousness is not a second-order faculty that observes mental states. In the strict present sense, consciousness is simply the occurrence of the present mental state. When consciousness is expanded into self-consciousness, it involves memory and the intuitive belief that present and past states belong to one identical mind.

The self is not discovered as an object among other objects. It is the permanent subject to which states are referred. Its continued identity is an intuitive belief, not a demonstrated theorem. This identity is central to Brown’s rejection of materialism: the absolute identity of the thinking principle cannot be reduced to the changing particles of the body.

Perception is sensation plus reference to an external cause. The belief in external things is not produced by a special faculty or by successful philosophical proof. It arises through touch, resistance, muscular experience, association, and intuitive causal expectation; once formed, it is practically irresistible.

The intellect is governed by suggestion. Simple suggestion explains the spontaneous succession of conceptions; relative suggestion explains judgment, comparison, relation, and reasoning. Memory, recollection, imagination, and composition are complex formations out of suggestion, desire, relation, and judgment, not primitive faculties.

Emotion is a fundamental class of mental phenomena. Emotions are vivid internal states arising upon perception, memory, imagination, and comparison. They are essential to aesthetics, morality, social affection, and religious life. Brown’s systematic use of “emotion” marks a historically important step in the formation of modern psychological vocabulary.

Causation, including mental causation, is regular sequence. We do not perceive occult powers, either in matter or in will. We observe, or naturally expect, uniform antecedence and consequence. The mind’s own order is therefore a lawful order, even though the subject of that order is spiritual rather than material.

The soul is real, spiritual, and immortal, but not scientifically knowable in its essence. Brown’s theism gives his psychology moral and religious depth, yet his empiricism restrains speculation about incorporeal beings. He affirms the human soul far more confidently than he theorizes angels or other immaterial minds.

20. Main tensions and philosophical evaluation

The first tension is between phenomenal method and substance dualism. Brown says we know only states and sequences, yet he affirms a permanent spiritual subject. His answer is that the subject is not known by analysis of essence but believed by an irresistible principle of our nature. Whether this is satisfactory depends on how much weight one grants to primitive belief in philosophy.

The second tension is in his anti-materialism. Brown’s argument from mental identity works best against materialisms that require identity to be identity of particles. It is less decisive against later forms of functionalism, emergentism, process physicalism, or psychological-continuity theories that do not require bodily particles to remain the same. Still, historically, his argument is a clear and important early nineteenth-century defense of the indivisible thinking subject against reductive material organization.

The third tension is in his account of perception. Brown wants to explain external-world belief by association and intuitive causal expectation, but the transition from interrupted muscular sequence to “something not-self” is philosophically delicate. He is persuasive as a psychologist of how externality becomes irresistible, but less decisive as a metaphysician proving external objects. In fairness, he does not claim that philosophical proof is the source of the belief.

The fourth tension concerns reduction of faculties. Brown’s analyses of memory, imagination, and will are elegant, but they may under-describe the normative structure of reasoning, intentionality, and agency. To say that judgment is a felt relation explains part of its phenomenology, but it may not fully explain truth, justification, logical validity, or intentional reference. Brown’s psychology is stronger as descriptive analysis than as a complete theory of rational normativity.

The fifth tension is historical: Brown is simultaneously a precursor of empirical psychology and a moral-theological philosopher of the soul. Modern readers may be tempted to split these apart, treating the law-psychology as progressive and the soul-language as residue. That would misrepresent him. For Brown, the science of mental phenomena and the dignity of the immortal mind belong to one project: understanding the human being as a lawful, moral, affective, intellectual, spiritually significant subject.

Conclusion

Brown’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a state-based, law-governed, anti-faculty, introspective psychology grounded in a minimal doctrine of the permanent spiritual subject. He rejects scholastic essence-hunting, hidden necessary connections, reified faculties, inner idea-objects, and speculative angelology. He retains the mind, the self, intuitive belief, external-world realism, moral emotion, religious destiny, and the immortality of the soul. His place in the history of philosophy of mind is therefore pivotal: he carries Scottish “science of mind” away from Reid’s faculty realism and toward associationist and empirical psychology, while preserving a theistic and anti-materialist metaphysics of the thinking subject.

PreviousJohann Friedrich Herbart NextBernard Bolzano

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
